Elevii și studenții eligibili își pot depune cererile și dosarele cu actele doveditoare până la data de 15 mai.

romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Nonprofit hospitals spend billions on management consultants... with no clear effect
medical.summary: In recent decades, management consulting firms have become a fixture in the American health care system, wielding outsized influence compared to most other economic sectors. Hospitals navigating challenging financial and regulatory landscapes may call on these specialists for advice on strategic planning, cost-cutting, reorganizations, or revenue-boosting initiatives.

medical.title: Central nervous system a key player in diabetes pain
medical.summary: Changes within the central nervous system, including the brain and spinal cord, can be a key reason for pain in people with diabetes, and future treatments could focus on restoring the brain's pain blocking systems, according to new research.

medical.title: Many genes have been linked to autism—but a new study suggests it may be their path to the brain that matters
medical.summary: In recent years, scientists have identified hundreds of different genes associated with autism, a burst of discovery that has prompted a new and perplexing question: how can so many different genes produce the same or very similar outcomes in the brain?

medical.title: Mistletoe extract shows no effect in patients with advanced pancreatic cancer
medical.summary: New research shows that palliative patients with pancreatic cancer neither live longer nor experience improved quality of life when taking mistletoe extract in addition to standard cancer treatment, compared to placebo. The study is published in the journal Palliative Medicine.

medical.title: One missing metabolic step can turn cancer's DNA-copying machinery into a lethal weakness
medical.summary: Loss of an enzyme necessary for a process called lipoylation disrupts the way cancer cells copy their DNA, increasing their vulnerability to a class of anticancer drugs known as PARP inhibitors, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers shows.

medical.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
medical.summary: For decades, relaxor ferroelectrics have powered everything from medical ultrasounds to sonar systems, yet their inner atomic structure remained a mystery—until now. Researchers have finally mapped their three-dimensional structure in unprecedented detail, uncovering hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale. The breakthrough not only challenges long-standing assumptions about how these materials behave but also allows scientists to refine the models used to design them.

medical.title: Cranberry juice may boost UTI antibiotics
medical.summary: More than 400 million people experience a urinary tract infection every year, and some epidemiological studies estimate that more than half of all women will develop at least one in their lifetime. Most UTIs are caused by pathogenic strains of Escherichia coli bacteria, and the antibiotic fosfomycin is often prescribed as the first line of treatment. However, increasing resistance to antibiotics is driving a search for alternative approaches to treatment.

medical.title: Hip replacement beats exercise-only care for moderate-to-severe hip arthritis in yearlong trial
medical.summary: Total hip arthroplasty leads to the best outcomes in the treatment of moderate-to-severe hip osteoarthritis, according to a new study published in The Bone & Joint Journal by the University of Eastern Finland and Kuopio University Hospital. During a one-year follow-up, surgery combined with conservative treatment was found to relieve pain and improve functional capacity and quality of life more effectively than conservative treatment alone, which was based on an exercise program.

medical.title: Proteins found in cancer extracellular vesicles could guide drug development
medical.summary: Cancer cells release tiny biological packages called extracellular vesicles (EVs) that help tumors grow and spread. A team led by Cedars-Sinai Health Sciences University investigators has now connected certain proteins carried in these EVs with poor patient outcomes in multiple cancer types. Their findings were published in the Journal of Extracellular Vesicles.

medical.title: Scientists reveal creatine’s hidden power beyond muscle gains
medical.summary: Creatine might be famous in the gym, but its real story is far more interesting. Naturally produced in the body, it helps power cells by rapidly regenerating ATP—the fuel that keeps muscles, the brain, and even the heart running during intense activity. Supplementing with creatine can boost short bursts of physical performance and may even support memory, mood, and cognitive speed, especially in people with lower baseline levels.

medical.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
medical.summary: A new study suggests depression may soon be detectable through a simple blood test—by tracking how certain immune cells age. Researchers found that accelerated aging in monocytes, a type of white blood cell, is closely tied to the emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression, like hopelessness and loss of pleasure, rather than physical symptoms such as fatigue.

science.title: How plants make copies of themselves—key 'cloning switch' gene identified
science.summary: A Hiroshima-University-led research team has discovered a key gene responsible for the initiation of gemma development, acting as a "master switch" to start asexual reproduction (cloning) in the model plant Marchantia polymorpha (common liverwort).

science.title: Does your child only read graphic novels? That's OK—it's helping them build literacy skills
science.summary: Some parents worry if their children only read graphic novels—or even mostly read them. A common question goes something like: how do I get my child to read something other than comics or graphic novels? But the answer might be: you don't have to.

science.title: A tiny world beyond Neptune has an atmosphere that shouldn't exist
science.summary: A team of professional and amateur Japanese astronomers have found evidence for a thin atmosphere around a small body in the outer solar system. The object is so small that it should not have a sustainable atmosphere, raising questions about when and how the atmosphere formed. Future observations to better characterize the atmosphere will help solve these mysteries.

science.title: Astronomers uncover over 1,000 radio galaxies with 'wings,' expanding a rare cosmic class
science.summary: Astronomers recently carried out a comprehensive search for strange "winged" radio galaxies using data from the LOFAR Two-meter Sky Survey Data Release 2 (LoTSS DR2) and discovered over 1,000 new systems. The paper outlining these results was submitted to the arXiv preprint server on April 24, 2026.

science.title: Overseas-owned properties shift from single homes to larger developments
science.summary: The number of luxury, detached homes in England and Wales owned through offshore companies has fallen dramatically in the last decade, likely as a result of a government transparency drive, UCL researchers say.

science.title: Is the Large Magellanic Cloud a first-time visitor?
science.summary: Our most massive satellite galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), has been the center of a heated debate in the astrophysics community over the last few years. That debate centers on whether this is the LMC's first or second "pass" by the Milky Way itself—and it has huge implications for the evolution of our galaxy given the disruption such a large grouping of stars has. A new paper from Scott Lucchini, Jiwon Jesse Han, Sapna Mishra, and Andrew J. Fox and his co-authors, currently available on the arXiv preprint server, provides what they claim to be definitive evidence that this is, in fact, the first time LMC has encountered the Milky Way.

science.title: Plants under stress switch from photosynthesis to protein cleanup, researchers show
science.summary: Plants are under constant stress due to pathogens, heat, or other environmental factors. Proteins can become damaged as a result and cell function is thrown off balance. Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum working with Professor Şuayb Üstün have discovered how plant cells respond to this protein stress and selectively adjust their internal processes. The researchers show that cells under stress prioritize the breaking down of damaged proteins over energy production through photosynthesis. Their findings, published in Molecular Cell, could help make plants more robust.

science.title: The Big Bang of plant life: Discovery sheds light on how cells form walls
science.summary: Cell walls are a crucial structure of plant life, protecting cells from damage, giving plants shape, and containing energy-rich nutrients. And yet the process of how the walls begin to form remains mysterious.

science.title: A cut, a color and the burden of care work
science.summary: Hair and beauty salons are spaces where we go to look and feel good. We have a bad day, a bad week, a bad breakup—we go to the salon. But ask any hair or beauty worker about what their job involves and they'll tell you the work to help clients feel good goes well beyond cutting hair or painting nails.

science.title: Scientists turn plastic waste into clean hydrogen fuel using sunlight
science.summary: Scientists are using sunlight to turn plastic waste into clean fuels like hydrogen, offering a breakthrough solution to both pollution and energy challenges. While still in development, the approach could transform trash into a valuable resource for a low-carbon future.

science.title: For NASA’s TESS, Stellar Eclipses Shed Light on Possible New Worlds
science.summary: A study of NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) data on stellar pairs undergoing mutual eclipses has uncovered more than two dozen candidate exoplanets, or worlds beyond our solar system. This method allows the mission to locate planets it couldn’t otherwise detect. To date, TESS has discovered 885 confirmed exoplanets and identified more than 7,900 […]

science.title: Glowing nanoparticles exposed hidden cancer-protein behavior that could reshape drug screening
science.summary: Using a powerful single-molecule imaging method they developed, a Broad Institute research team has unveiled a dynamic view of how some cancer-related proteins interact in living cells. The technique relies on highly stable nanoparticle probes that brightly illuminate individual molecules for long periods of time. The researchers used their method to observe, for the first time, individual receptors as they move around the cell membrane, attaching to and then letting go of other receptors to alter signaling within the cell.

science.title: LAGEOS: An Earth Science Mission Built for Enduring Precision
science.summary: On May 4, 1976, a spacecraft resembling a disco ball entered orbit almost 3,700 miles (6,000 kilometers) above Earth. This shiny, two‑foot‑wide (60‑centimeter) sphere called the Laser Geodynamics Satellite, or LAGEOS, is covered with 426 retroreflectors—small mirrored prisms designed to bounce laser light directly back to where it came from. Beneath its aluminum exterior sits […]

science.title: Clearing contamination from car interiors
science.summary: Under controlled conditions, Flinders University researchers are investigating third-hand contamination risk from methamphetamine use. Environmental health experts have found open windows and ventilation can reduce but not completely remove methamphetamine contamination on most hard surfaces of a car after an extended period.

science.title: Chromatin tracking reveals two motion modes that help control gene expression
science.summary: Gene expression is controlled, in part, by the interactions between genes and regulatory elements located along the genome. Those interactions depend on the ability of chromatin—a mix of DNA and proteins—to move around within a crowded space. In a new study, MIT researchers have measured chromatin movement at timescales ranging from hundreds of microseconds to hours, allowing them to rigorously quantify those dynamics for the first time.

science.title: The da Vinci bloodline is unlocking the genius’s genetic secrets
science.summary: After centuries of mystery, scientists are edging closer to uncovering Leonardo da Vinci’s biological secrets. A massive 30-year effort has mapped his family across 21 generations, identified living male descendants, and even confirmed shared DNA markers that stretch back to the Renaissance. Meanwhile, excavations at a family tomb in Vinci could provide ancient remains for comparison, raising the possibility of reconstructing Leonardo’s genetic profile.

science.title: Close-in planets act as 'bouncers' to create rogue worlds
science.summary: Rogue planets sound like rare travelers among the stars, freed from the gravitational constraints of a host system, left to forever wander the interstellar void. But modern models suggest these free floating planets (FFPs) as they are technically known, are actually very common—19 times more common than planets beyond the "snow line," which is the distance from the central star where it becomes cold enough that hydrogen compounds like water, ammonia, and methane can condense into ice.

science.title: Live camel transportation improved by using food as an incentive in place of physical punishment
science.summary: Around the world, millions of camels are farmed for milk and meat while others are used in leisure activities like racing and riding. Yet the treatment of these animals as livestock can be harsh, especially during transport. New international research, led by Southern Cross University animal welfare expert Associate Professor Barbara Padalino and published in the journal Animals, shows that more humane camel transportation is possible by using food as an incentive in place of physical punishment.

science.title: Arctic winter sea-ice extent fails to expand and sets a new record low in 2026
science.summary: In 2026, the Arctic winter sea-ice extent (annual maximum extent) reached the lowest value since satellite observations began in 1979, following the previous record low in March 2025. As part of the Arctic Challenge for Sustainability III (ArCS III), the National Institute of Polar Research (NIPR) and the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) maintain a long-term dataset spanning more than 40 years.

science.title: Speed 'training' prepares bacteria for complex tasks, like munching plastics
science.summary: Millions of tons of plastic waste accumulate in landfills and oceans every year. One promising response is to engineer microbes to break the plastic down into useful chemical building blocks. However, teaching a bacterium to digest plastic efficiently demands fine-tuning not just one gene, but entire clusters of genes working in concert, like upgrading every machine on a factory assembly line rather than swapping out a single part.

science.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
science.summary: New experiments suggest that freezing and thawing on early Earth may have helped primitive cell-like structures grow and evolve. Tiny lipid bubbles behaved very differently depending on their membrane makeup—some fused into larger compartments and captured DNA more efficiently. These fusion events could have mixed key molecules, setting the stage for more complex chemistry.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Programul național de educație muzicală BeatAgora anunță lansarea primei ediții a Concursului Național de Tobe – BeatAgora, o competiție dedicată tinerilor muzicieni din școlile și liceele din România
mix_items.summary: Programul național de educație muzicală BeatAgora anunță lansarea primei ediții a Concursului Național de Tobe – BeatAgora, o competiție dedicată tinerilor muzicieni din școlile și liceele din România. Concursul se… Articolul Programul național de educație muzicală BeatAgora anunță lansarea primei ediții a Concursului Național de Tobe – BeatAgora, o competiție dedicată tinerilor muzicieni din școlile și liceele din România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Elevii și studenții pot primi bani pentru calculatoare în 2026: Venitul eligibil a fost majorat. Până când se depun cererile
mix_items.summary: Ministerul Educației a publicat calendarul oficial pentru acordarea ajutorului financiar destinat achiziționării de calculatoare în 2026, în cadrul programului „Euro 200”. Elevii și studenții eligibili își pot depune cererile și dosarele cu actele doveditoare până la data de 15 mai.

mix_items.title: Studiu Maratonul Olteniei: Românii fac sport pentru sănătate, iar comunitățile îi țin motivați
mix_items.summary: Conceptul #SportiviDeGașcă, care definește ediția din acest an a Maratonului Olteniei, un eveniment organizat de Distribuție Oltenia, este susținut de datele studiului* „Cum se antrenează românii și ce îi ține… Articolul Studiu Maratonul Olteniei: Românii fac sport pentru sănătate, iar comunitățile îi țin motivați apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Nou local în Bucureşti, inspirat de o celebră damă de companie: La Mă-ta Biciclista
mix_items.summary: Un nou lăcaş de cultură cu merdenele de fiţe face furori în high-life-ul bucureştean! Ca şi în cazul mai cunoscutei Miţa, localul proaspăt inaugurat e botezat după o celebră damă de companie din vechime: La Mă-ta Biciclista. „Asemenea Miţei, Mă-ta Biciclista era o adevărată femeie fatală a epocii, cunoscută pentru apetitul ei sexual şi viaţa […] The post Nou local în Bucureşti, inspirat de o celebră damă de companie: La Mă-ta Biciclista appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Fostul proprietar Regina Maria revine în România cu o nouă achiziție
mix_items.summary: Fondul de investiții MidEuropa, care recent a cedat în România rețeaua de centre medicale Regina Maria, cumpără acum pachetul majoritar de acțiuni al Romanian Business Consult (RBC), integrator IT specializat in sectoarele de retail, banking și producție industrială din România. Fondatorul și CEO-ul companiei, Andrei Bojiță, va păstra un pachet semnificativ de acțiuni și va …

mix_items.title: Avertisment din sectorul energetic: soluțiile propuse de ANRE la problema numărului mare de avize vor determina scumpirea energiei, nu ieftinirea ei
mix_items.summary: Investitorii din sectorul energiei regenerabile susțin că măsurile propuse de autorități pentru reducerea numărului mare de avize, care este o problemă reală, va avea efectul invers decât cel așteptat și va determina creșteri de prețuri, în loc să contribuie la reducerea lor. Asociația Română pentru Energie Eoliană (RWEA) și Asociația Industriei Fotovoltaice din România (RPIA) …

mix_items.title: Festivalul Filmului Palestinian revine la București cu ediția a VI-a între 8 și 10 mai
mix_items.summary: Festivalul Filmului Palestinian revine la București cu cea de-a șasea ediție, care va avea loc între 8 și 10 mai 2026, la Cinema Muzeul Țăranului. Evenimentul propune o selecție de… Articolul Festivalul Filmului Palestinian revine la București cu ediția a VI-a între 8 și 10 mai apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: The official soundtrack of Electroruga 2026 – full lineup & daily schedule. Go all in with a full pass and live the festival the way it was meant to be… Articolul The official soundtrack of Electroruga 2026 – full lineup apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Brașov, 04 mai 2026. Turiștii care vizitează Munții Făgăraș au acum la dispoziție o platformă care reunește Punctele Gastronomice Locale din zonă. Fundația Conservation Carpathia, prin divizia Roadele Munților, lansează… Articolul Unde mănânci direct la gazdă: S-a lansat harta Punctelor Gastronomice Locale din sudul Munților Făgăraș apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: TNTm: Ion Rizea este noul manager interimar al Teatrului Național „Mihai Eminescu” Timișoara
mix_items.summary: Începând de vineri, 1 mai 2026, actorul Ion Rizea, director general adjunct al teatrului de aproape 20 de ani, a preluat atribuțiile de conducere și reprezentare a Teatrului Național „Mihai… Articolul TNTm: Ion Rizea este noul manager interimar al Teatrului Național „Mihai Eminescu” Timișoara apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Pe 13–14 iunie 2026, la Ferma Dacilor, în zona Dealu Mare, va avea loc evenimentul „Târg Gastronomic Experiențial – VATRĂ ȘI OGRADĂ, de la gospodari la gurmanzi”. Vatră și Ogradă transformă produsul local în experiență gastronomică de weekend apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Coiful de la Coțofenești va fi restaurat. Va primi Wifi și căști JBL
mix_items.summary: După ce a fost recuperat din Olanda, coiful dacic de aur de la Coțofenești a ajuns la Muzeul Național de Istorie și urmează să intre în restaurare masivă ca să fie adus la standardele cu care sunt obișnuiți pasionații de istorie din prezent. Celebrul coif dacic de la Coțofenești va beneficia de câteva îmbunătățiri esențiale. […] The post Coiful de la Coțofenești va fi restaurat. Va primi Wifi și căști JBL appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: How plants make copies of themselves—key 'cloning switch' gene identified
mix_items.summary: A Hiroshima-University-led research team has discovered a key gene responsible for the initiation of gemma development, acting as a "master switch" to start asexual reproduction (cloning) in the model plant Marchantia polymorpha (common liverwort).

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Does your child only read graphic novels? That's OK—it's helping them build literacy skills
mix_items.summary: Some parents worry if their children only read graphic novels—or even mostly read them. A common question goes something like: how do I get my child to read something other than comics or graphic novels? But the answer might be: you don't have to.

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: A tiny world beyond Neptune has an atmosphere that shouldn't exist
mix_items.summary: A team of professional and amateur Japanese astronomers have found evidence for a thin atmosphere around a small body in the outer solar system. The object is so small that it should not have a sustainable atmosphere, raising questions about when and how the atmosphere formed. Future observations to better characterize the atmosphere will help solve these mysteries.

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Nonprofit hospitals spend billions on management consultants... with no clear effect
mix_items.summary: In recent decades, management consulting firms have become a fixture in the American health care system, wielding outsized influence compared to most other economic sectors. Hospitals navigating challenging financial and regulatory landscapes may call on these specialists for advice on strategic planning, cost-cutting, reorganizations, or revenue-boosting initiatives.

mix_items.title: Astronomers uncover over 1,000 radio galaxies with 'wings,' expanding a rare cosmic class
mix_items.summary: Astronomers recently carried out a comprehensive search for strange "winged" radio galaxies using data from the LOFAR Two-meter Sky Survey Data Release 2 (LoTSS DR2) and discovered over 1,000 new systems. The paper outlining these results was submitted to the arXiv preprint server on April 24, 2026.

mix_items.title: Central nervous system a key player in diabetes pain
mix_items.summary: Changes within the central nervous system, including the brain and spinal cord, can be a key reason for pain in people with diabetes, and future treatments could focus on restoring the brain's pain blocking systems, according to new research.

mix_items.title: Overseas-owned properties shift from single homes to larger developments
mix_items.summary: The number of luxury, detached homes in England and Wales owned through offshore companies has fallen dramatically in the last decade, likely as a result of a government transparency drive, UCL researchers say.

mix_items.title: Many genes have been linked to autism—but a new study suggests it may be their path to the brain that matters
mix_items.summary: In recent years, scientists have identified hundreds of different genes associated with autism, a burst of discovery that has prompted a new and perplexing question: how can so many different genes produce the same or very similar outcomes in the brain?

mix_items.title: Is the Large Magellanic Cloud a first-time visitor?
mix_items.summary: Our most massive satellite galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), has been the center of a heated debate in the astrophysics community over the last few years. That debate centers on whether this is the LMC's first or second "pass" by the Milky Way itself—and it has huge implications for the evolution of our galaxy given the disruption such a large grouping of stars has. A new paper from Scott Lucchini, Jiwon Jesse Han, Sapna Mishra, and Andrew J. Fox and his co-authors, currently available on the arXiv preprint server, provides what they claim to be definitive evidence that this is, in fact, the first time LMC has encountered the Milky Way.

mix_items.title: Plants under stress switch from photosynthesis to protein cleanup, researchers show
mix_items.summary: Plants are under constant stress due to pathogens, heat, or other environmental factors. Proteins can become damaged as a result and cell function is thrown off balance. Researchers at Ruhr University Bochum working with Professor Şuayb Üstün have discovered how plant cells respond to this protein stress and selectively adjust their internal processes. The researchers show that cells under stress prioritize the breaking down of damaged proteins over energy production through photosynthesis. Their findings, published in Molecular Cell, could help make plants more robust.

mix_items.title: Mistletoe extract shows no effect in patients with advanced pancreatic cancer
mix_items.summary: New research shows that palliative patients with pancreatic cancer neither live longer nor experience improved quality of life when taking mistletoe extract in addition to standard cancer treatment, compared to placebo. The study is published in the journal Palliative Medicine.

mix_items.title: The Big Bang of plant life: Discovery sheds light on how cells form walls
mix_items.summary: Cell walls are a crucial structure of plant life, protecting cells from damage, giving plants shape, and containing energy-rich nutrients. And yet the process of how the walls begin to form remains mysterious.

mix_items.title: A cut, a color and the burden of care work
mix_items.summary: Hair and beauty salons are spaces where we go to look and feel good. We have a bad day, a bad week, a bad breakup—we go to the salon. But ask any hair or beauty worker about what their job involves and they'll tell you the work to help clients feel good goes well beyond cutting hair or painting nails.

mix_items.title: Scientists turn plastic waste into clean hydrogen fuel using sunlight
mix_items.summary: Scientists are using sunlight to turn plastic waste into clean fuels like hydrogen, offering a breakthrough solution to both pollution and energy challenges. While still in development, the approach could transform trash into a valuable resource for a low-carbon future.

mix_items.title: For NASA’s TESS, Stellar Eclipses Shed Light on Possible New Worlds
mix_items.summary: A study of NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) data on stellar pairs undergoing mutual eclipses has uncovered more than two dozen candidate exoplanets, or worlds beyond our solar system. This method allows the mission to locate planets it couldn’t otherwise detect. To date, TESS has discovered 885 confirmed exoplanets and identified more than 7,900 […]

mix_items.title: Glowing nanoparticles exposed hidden cancer-protein behavior that could reshape drug screening
mix_items.summary: Using a powerful single-molecule imaging method they developed, a Broad Institute research team has unveiled a dynamic view of how some cancer-related proteins interact in living cells. The technique relies on highly stable nanoparticle probes that brightly illuminate individual molecules for long periods of time. The researchers used their method to observe, for the first time, individual receptors as they move around the cell membrane, attaching to and then letting go of other receptors to alter signaling within the cell.

mix_items.title: One missing metabolic step can turn cancer's DNA-copying machinery into a lethal weakness
mix_items.summary: Loss of an enzyme necessary for a process called lipoylation disrupts the way cancer cells copy their DNA, increasing their vulnerability to a class of anticancer drugs known as PARP inhibitors, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers shows.

mix_items.title: LAGEOS: An Earth Science Mission Built for Enduring Precision
mix_items.summary: On May 4, 1976, a spacecraft resembling a disco ball entered orbit almost 3,700 miles (6,000 kilometers) above Earth. This shiny, two‑foot‑wide (60‑centimeter) sphere called the Laser Geodynamics Satellite, or LAGEOS, is covered with 426 retroreflectors—small mirrored prisms designed to bounce laser light directly back to where it came from. Beneath its aluminum exterior sits […]

mix_items.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
mix_items.summary: For decades, relaxor ferroelectrics have powered everything from medical ultrasounds to sonar systems, yet their inner atomic structure remained a mystery—until now. Researchers have finally mapped their three-dimensional structure in unprecedented detail, uncovering hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale. The breakthrough not only challenges long-standing assumptions about how these materials behave but also allows scientists to refine the models used to design them.

mix_items.title: Cranberry juice may boost UTI antibiotics
mix_items.summary: More than 400 million people experience a urinary tract infection every year, and some epidemiological studies estimate that more than half of all women will develop at least one in their lifetime. Most UTIs are caused by pathogenic strains of Escherichia coli bacteria, and the antibiotic fosfomycin is often prescribed as the first line of treatment. However, increasing resistance to antibiotics is driving a search for alternative approaches to treatment.

mix_items.title: Clearing contamination from car interiors
mix_items.summary: Under controlled conditions, Flinders University researchers are investigating third-hand contamination risk from methamphetamine use. Environmental health experts have found open windows and ventilation can reduce but not completely remove methamphetamine contamination on most hard surfaces of a car after an extended period.

mix_items.title: Chromatin tracking reveals two motion modes that help control gene expression
mix_items.summary: Gene expression is controlled, in part, by the interactions between genes and regulatory elements located along the genome. Those interactions depend on the ability of chromatin—a mix of DNA and proteins—to move around within a crowded space. In a new study, MIT researchers have measured chromatin movement at timescales ranging from hundreds of microseconds to hours, allowing them to rigorously quantify those dynamics for the first time.

mix_items.title: The da Vinci bloodline is unlocking the genius’s genetic secrets
mix_items.summary: After centuries of mystery, scientists are edging closer to uncovering Leonardo da Vinci’s biological secrets. A massive 30-year effort has mapped his family across 21 generations, identified living male descendants, and even confirmed shared DNA markers that stretch back to the Renaissance. Meanwhile, excavations at a family tomb in Vinci could provide ancient remains for comparison, raising the possibility of reconstructing Leonardo’s genetic profile.

mix_items.title: Close-in planets act as 'bouncers' to create rogue worlds
mix_items.summary: Rogue planets sound like rare travelers among the stars, freed from the gravitational constraints of a host system, left to forever wander the interstellar void. But modern models suggest these free floating planets (FFPs) as they are technically known, are actually very common—19 times more common than planets beyond the "snow line," which is the distance from the central star where it becomes cold enough that hydrogen compounds like water, ammonia, and methane can condense into ice.

mix_items.title: Hip replacement beats exercise-only care for moderate-to-severe hip arthritis in yearlong trial
mix_items.summary: Total hip arthroplasty leads to the best outcomes in the treatment of moderate-to-severe hip osteoarthritis, according to a new study published in The Bone & Joint Journal by the University of Eastern Finland and Kuopio University Hospital. During a one-year follow-up, surgery combined with conservative treatment was found to relieve pain and improve functional capacity and quality of life more effectively than conservative treatment alone, which was based on an exercise program.

mix_items.title: Live camel transportation improved by using food as an incentive in place of physical punishment
mix_items.summary: Around the world, millions of camels are farmed for milk and meat while others are used in leisure activities like racing and riding. Yet the treatment of these animals as livestock can be harsh, especially during transport. New international research, led by Southern Cross University animal welfare expert Associate Professor Barbara Padalino and published in the journal Animals, shows that more humane camel transportation is possible by using food as an incentive in place of physical punishment.

mix_items.title: Arctic winter sea-ice extent fails to expand and sets a new record low in 2026
mix_items.summary: In 2026, the Arctic winter sea-ice extent (annual maximum extent) reached the lowest value since satellite observations began in 1979, following the previous record low in March 2025. As part of the Arctic Challenge for Sustainability III (ArCS III), the National Institute of Polar Research (NIPR) and the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) maintain a long-term dataset spanning more than 40 years.

mix_items.title: Proteins found in cancer extracellular vesicles could guide drug development
mix_items.summary: Cancer cells release tiny biological packages called extracellular vesicles (EVs) that help tumors grow and spread. A team led by Cedars-Sinai Health Sciences University investigators has now connected certain proteins carried in these EVs with poor patient outcomes in multiple cancer types. Their findings were published in the Journal of Extracellular Vesicles.

mix_items.title: Speed 'training' prepares bacteria for complex tasks, like munching plastics
mix_items.summary: Millions of tons of plastic waste accumulate in landfills and oceans every year. One promising response is to engineer microbes to break the plastic down into useful chemical building blocks. However, teaching a bacterium to digest plastic efficiently demands fine-tuning not just one gene, but entire clusters of genes working in concert, like upgrading every machine on a factory assembly line rather than swapping out a single part.

mix_items.title: Scientists reveal creatine’s hidden power beyond muscle gains
mix_items.summary: Creatine might be famous in the gym, but its real story is far more interesting. Naturally produced in the body, it helps power cells by rapidly regenerating ATP—the fuel that keeps muscles, the brain, and even the heart running during intense activity. Supplementing with creatine can boost short bursts of physical performance and may even support memory, mood, and cognitive speed, especially in people with lower baseline levels.

mix_items.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
mix_items.summary: A new study suggests depression may soon be detectable through a simple blood test—by tracking how certain immune cells age. Researchers found that accelerated aging in monocytes, a type of white blood cell, is closely tied to the emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression, like hopelessness and loss of pleasure, rather than physical symptoms such as fatigue.

mix_items.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
mix_items.summary: New experiments suggest that freezing and thawing on early Earth may have helped primitive cell-like structures grow and evolve. Tiny lipid bubbles behaved very differently depending on their membrane makeup—some fused into larger compartments and captured DNA more efficiently. These fusion events could have mixed key molecules, setting the stage for more complex chemistry.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Telefonul are 3% baterie, dar incredere maxima in mine.

satire_ro.title: Kremlinul neagă că Putin se ascunde în buncăr de ucrainieni: „Se ascunde de Șoșoacă!”
